our_argument_summary: Our review says the 11-12 Hz band is an 'attenuation valley' between Schumann peaks, that MT geophysicists 'deliberately avoid this band,' and cites a Springer DOI (10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4) as MT survey literature. We classify this as Refuted by Data.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Completely static. The domain entry hardcodes predicted=11.7, observed=11.7, error_pct=0.0, pass=True. No data is fetched, no spectrum is analyzed, no comparison is made against any dataset. The 'observed' value is identical to the 'predicted' value by construction.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Everything is hardcoded. Both predicted and observed values are static strings. The 5.0% tolerance is irrelevant since error is hardcoded at 0.0%. The pass=True is a literal boolean, not the result of any comparison.
data_sources_used: None. Source field says 'IRIS / Geometrics MT data (WIN-008 static win)' but no IRIS or Geometrics API is called. The parenthetical '(WIN-008 static win)' is telling — the code itself labels this a static assertion.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None needed — the entry always passes by construction.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review doesn't explicitly note that the dome's own code labels this a 'static win' in its source field. This is a useful detail we could add — the dome's own codebase admits this isn't a computed validation.

holes_found.description: WRONG DOI CITATION: Our review links both WIN-008 and WIN-009 to DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4 with anchor text 'Magnetotelluric (MT)'. This DOI actually resolves to 'Mathematical Methods in Electro-Magneto-Elasticity' by Bardzokas, Filshtinsky & Filshtinsky (Springer, Lecture Notes in Applied and Computational Mechanics vol. 32) — a book about mathematical methods in elastic media, NOT about magnetotelluric surveys. This is a verifiable factual error that undermines our credibility. Anyone who clicks the link will find an unrelated book.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace with a correct MT reference. Good options: (1) Chave & Jones (2012) 'The Magnetotelluric Method: Theory and Practice' (Cambridge UP), which discusses natural source spectra; (2) Simpson & Bahr (2005) 'Practical Magnetotellurics' (Cambridge UP); (3) Geometrics FAQ page at geometrics.com/support/what-natural-magnetotelluric-frequencies-exist/ which directly discusses the dead band at 800 Hz–4 kHz.

holes_found.description: CONFLATION OF DEAD BAND WITH INTER-HARMONIC MINIMUM: Our review says 'working geophysicists conducting MT surveys deliberately avoid this band because it is an attenuation valley.' The well-documented MT 'dead band' (also called the AMT dead band) that geophysicists actually avoid is at 800 Hz–5 kHz, NOT at 11-12 Hz. The 11-12 Hz region is between Schumann harmonics and does have lower natural EM power than the peaks, but it is not the formal 'dead band' of MT practice. Our language conflates two different phenomena.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite to be precise: 'The 11-12 Hz region falls between the Schumann fundamental (~7.83 Hz) and first harmonic (~14.3 Hz), where the natural electromagnetic spectrum has a local power minimum. This is a standard feature of the spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity resonance spectrum, not evidence of a disc resonance ceiling.' Drop the 'geophysicists deliberately avoid' claim or, if retaining it, correctly attribute it to the 1-5 kHz band.

holes_found.description: We say 'the claim inverts the actual spectrum' — this is slightly imprecise. The claim asserts a 'sharp cutoff' and our review says it's an 'attenuation valley.' Both are wrong: it's an inter-harmonic minimum, which is neither a cutoff nor a valley caused by attenuation. A cutoff would mean no signal above that frequency; a valley implies active attenuation. What actually exists is simply lower power between resonance peaks.
holes_found.recommendation: Sharpen language: the 11-12 Hz region has lower spectral power density than the Schumann peaks because it lies between cavity harmonics, but there is no 'sharp cutoff' — the first Schumann harmonic at ~14.3 Hz has robust power, directly disproving any cutoff at 11.7 Hz.

stronger_arguments.description: THE 14.3 Hz HARMONIC DISPROOF: The most devastating counter to a 'cutoff at 11.7 Hz' is simply that the first Schumann harmonic at ~14.3 Hz exists and is routinely measured worldwide. If there were a genuine resonance ceiling at 11.7 Hz, no electromagnetic energy could sustain cavity modes above that frequency. The well-documented harmonics at 14.3, 20.8, 27.3, and 33.8 Hz directly falsify any cutoff claim. This is simpler and more powerful than our current argument about MT survey practices.
sources: Schumann resonance measurements at any monitoring station (e.g., Tomsk, Hylaty, Nagycenk)
Wikipedia: Schumann_resonances — lists all five harmonics

stronger_arguments.description: THE SPHERICAL CAVITY PREDICTION: The inter-harmonic minimum at 11-12 Hz is a PREDICTION of the standard spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity model. The Schumann resonances arise from electromagnetic standing waves in the spherical cavity between Earth's surface and the ionosphere, and the standard model predicts exactly where the power minima fall. The dome model is citing a feature of spherical-cavity physics as evidence for its own model — this is another instance of the 'relabeling standard physics' pattern identified in our review.
sources: Nickolaenko & Hayakawa (2002) 'Resonances in the Earth-Ionosphere Cavity' (Springer)

stronger_arguments.description: THE CODE SELF-LABELS IT STATIC: The dome's own monitor.py labels WIN-008 as '(WIN-008 static win)' in the source field. This is an admission that this is not a computed or validated prediction — it's a manually asserted match. We could quote this directly.

kernel_of_truth: There IS genuinely lower spectral power density in the 11-12 Hz range of the natural ELF electromagnetic spectrum. This is real — it's the inter-harmonic minimum between the Schumann fundamental at ~7.83 Hz and the first harmonic at ~14.3 Hz. The dome model correctly identifies that this frequency region has less electromagnetic energy. However, this is fully explained by the standard spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity resonance model. The inter-harmonic minima are a natural consequence of any resonant cavity, and the spherical model predicts them precisely. The dome model's error is calling this an 'engineered cutoff' at a specific frequency matching its disc resonance formula, when it's actually a smooth power minimum predicted by standard physics.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-008 claims a 'sharp telluric cutoff at 11.7 Hz' that matches the dome's disc resonance ceiling frequency, calculated from f = c/(2D) where D is disc thickness. The dome's own code in monitor.py reveals this is entirely a static assertion: domain #25 ('Telluric Resonance Cutoff') hardcodes both predicted and observed values as 11.7 Hz with 0.0% error and pass=True. No MT data is fetched from IRIS or Geometrics despite the source field claiming these datasets. The code itself parenthetically labels this a '(WIN-008 static win),' effectively admitting it's an assertion rather than a validated measurement.

Our review's verdict of 'Refuted by Data' is correct in substance, but our supporting argument has two problems that need fixing. First, our sole cited source — DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4, linked with anchor text 'Magnetotelluric (MT)' — actually resolves to 'Mathematical Methods in Electro-Magneto-Elasticity' by Bardzokas et al., a Springer book about mathematical methods in elastic media that has nothing to do with MT surveys. This is a straightforward citation error that any careful reader could catch, and it would undermine our credibility. Second, our claim that 'working geophysicists deliberately avoid this band' conflates the well-known AMT dead band (800 Hz–5 kHz, where natural EM source energy is weak due to waveguide dissipation) with the inter-harmonic minimum between Schumann peaks at ~8 Hz and ~14 Hz. These are different phenomena at different frequencies. The Geometrics FAQ page — the very source the dome model cites — describes the dead band at 800 Hz–4 kHz, not at 11-12 Hz.

A stronger rebuttal is available and much simpler: the first Schumann harmonic at ~14.3 Hz exists and is routinely measured at stations worldwide. If there were a genuine resonance 'cutoff' at 11.7 Hz, no cavity mode could sustain energy above that frequency, and the 14.3 Hz harmonic (plus the 20.8, 27.3, and 33.8 Hz harmonics) would not exist. They do exist. This single fact falsifies the 'cutoff' claim without any need to discuss MT survey practices. Additionally, the inter-harmonic power minimum between 8 and 14 Hz is itself a standard prediction of spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity physics — it's another instance of the dome relabeling globe predictions as its own.

Recommended edits: (1) Replace the broken DOI with a correct MT reference such as Chave & Jones (2012) or the Geometrics FAQ URL; (2) Replace the 'geophysicists deliberately avoid' language with a precise statement about the inter-harmonic minimum and the existence of the 14.3 Hz harmonic; (3) Optionally note that the dome's own code labels this a 'static win' with no actual data validation.
